questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As a horse pulls a cart, the force responsible for propelling the horse forward is the force applied by
questions.solution:
When a horse pulls a cart, it pushes the ground backward with its feet.

According to Newton's third law of motion, for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

The ground exerts an equal and opposite reaction force on the horse in the forward direction.

This reaction force applied by the ground on the horse is responsible for propelling the horse forward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a car accelerates along a horizontal road, the force responsible for the acceleration is exerted by
questions.solution:
When a car accelerates, the engine provides torque to the wheels, causing them to rotate.

As the wheels rotate, they push backward against the road due to friction.

According to Newton's third law of motion, the road exerts an equal and opposite forward frictional force on the tires.

Since internal forces cannot accelerate the center of mass of the system, this external frictional force exerted by the road is responsible for the acceleration of the car.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of weight $w_1$ is hung from the ceiling of a room via a chain of weight $w_2$. The ceiling pulls the chain with a force equal to
questions.solution:
Consider the body and the chain as a single system.

The total downward force acting on this system is the sum of their weights, which is $w_1 + w_2$.

Since the system is in equilibrium, the upward force exerted by the ceiling must balance the total downward force.

Therefore, the ceiling pulls the chain with an upward force equal to $w_1 + w_2$.

Alternatively, considering the free body diagram of the chain, the downward forces acting on it are its own weight $w_2$ and the downward pull from the body $w_1$.

Let the upward force exerted by the ceiling be $T$.

For equilibrium of the chain, $T = w_1 + w_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $m$ rests on a smooth inclined plane making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. What is the magnitude of the force exerted by the plane on the block?
questions.solution:
The forces acting on the block are its weight $mg$ acting vertically downwards and the normal reaction $N$ acting perpendicular to the inclined plane.

Resolving the weight $mg$ into two components: $mg \sin\theta$ parallel to the inclined plane and $mg \cos\theta$ perpendicular to the inclined plane.

Since there is no motion perpendicular to the inclined plane, the forces in this direction are balanced.

Therefore, the normal reaction $N$ exerted by the plane on the block is equal to the perpendicular component of the weight.

$N = mg \cos\theta$

Since the plane is smooth, there is no frictional force. Thus, the total force exerted by the plane on the block is $mg \cos\theta$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a block of mass $10\text{ kg}$ is suspended via two light spring balances.
options: The individual readings may vary, but their sum will always be $10\text{ kg}$.
Both scales will display a reading of $5\text{ kg}$.
The upper scale will read $10\text{ kg}$ and the lower scale will read zero.
Both scales will display a reading of $10\text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
Since the spring balances are light, their masses are considered negligible.

A spring balance measures the tension force acting on it.

For the lower spring balance, the tension $T_2$ is equal to the weight of the $10\text{ kg}$ block. Thus, $T_2 = 10g$, and its reading is $10\text{ kg}$.

For the upper spring balance, the tension $T_1$ must balance the total downward force, which is the tension from the lower spring balance plus the weight of the lower spring balance.

Since the lower spring balance is massless, $T_1 = T_2 = 10g$.

Therefore, the upper spring balance also reads $10\text{ kg}$.

Both scales will display a reading of $10\text{ kg}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $m$ is positioned on a smooth wedge having an inclination $	heta$. The entire system is accelerated horizontally such that the block does not slip on the wedge. The magnitude of the force exerted on the block by the wedge is
questions.solution:
The block does not slip on the wedge, which implies it moves horizontally with the same acceleration as the wedge.

The forces acting on the block are its weight $mg$ acting vertically downwards and the normal reaction $N$ exerted by the smooth wedge, acting perpendicular to the inclined surface.

Since there is no vertical acceleration of the block, the vertical component of the normal reaction balances the weight of the block.

Equating the vertical forces:

$N \cos\theta = mg$

$N = \dfrac{mg}{\cos\theta}$

The magnitude of the force exerted on the block by the wedge is the normal reaction $N$, which is $\dfrac{mg}{\cos\theta}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Disregarding the effect of the earth's rotation, imagine the earth abruptly ceases to attract objects positioned near its surface. A person standing on the surface of the earth would
options: move tangentially to the earth's surface
questions.solution:
Disregarding the effect of the earth's rotation, the initial velocity of the person standing on the earth's surface is zero.

When the earth abruptly ceases to attract objects, the gravitational force becomes zero. Consequently, the normal reaction force exerted by the surface also becomes zero.

Since no external force acts on the person, the net force is zero.

According to Newton's first law of motion, an object at rest remains at rest unless acted upon by an external force. Therefore, the person will maintain their state of rest and continue standing on the surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three rigid rods are connected to create an equilateral triangle $ABC$ of side $1 \text{ m}$. Three particles, each bearing a charge of $20 \,\mu\text{C}$, are fastened to the triangle's vertices. The complete system is stationary in an inertial frame. What is the magnitude of the resultant force experienced by the charged particle at $A$?
questions.solution:
Since the complete system is stationary in an inertial frame, the acceleration of each charged particle is zero.

According to Newton's first law of motion, the net (resultant) force acting on any stationary particle must be zero.

The resultant electrostatic force of repulsion on the particle at $A$ (which is $3.6\sqrt{3} \text{ N}$) is exactly balanced by the mechanical reaction forces exerted by the rigid rods to which it is fastened.

Therefore, the magnitude of the total resultant force experienced by the charged particle at $A$ is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force $F_1$ acts on a particle, accelerating it from rest to a velocity $v$. Subsequently, $F_1$ is replaced by a force $F_2$ that decelerates the particle back to rest.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the particle be $m$.

During the acceleration phase, the particle starts from rest and attains a velocity $v$. Let $t_1$ be the time for which the force $F_1$ acts. Using the impulse-momentum theorem, we have:

$F_1 t_1 = m v - 0 = m v$

During the deceleration phase, the particle is brought back to rest from velocity $v$. Let $t_2$ be the time for which the force $F_2$ acts. The magnitude of the change in momentum is the same. Thus, we have:

$F_2 t_2 = m v - 0 = m v$

Equating the two expressions, we get:

$F_1 t_1 = F_2 t_2$

Since the time intervals $t_1$ and $t_2$ are not specified in the problem, they can take any positive values.

If $t_1 = t_2$, then $F_1 = F_2$.

If $t_1 \neq t_2$, then $F_1 \neq F_2$.

Therefore, the magnitude of the force $F_1$ may or may not be equal to $F_2$. The correct statement is that $F_1$ may be equal to $F_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two objects $A$ and $B$ are projected upward at the same time with identical speeds. The mass of $A$ exceeds the mass of $B$. Assume that air applies a constant and equal resistive force on both bodies.
options: Any of these three outcomes could occur depending on the initial projection speed.
$B$ will reach a greater height than $A$.
The two bodies will attain identical heights.
$A$ will reach a greater height than $B$.
questions.solution:
Let the initial speed of both objects be $u$.

The retardation of an object of mass $m$ moving upwards under gravity and a constant air resistance $F$ is given by $a = g + \dfrac{F}{m}$.

For object $A$, $a_A = g + \dfrac{F}{m_A}$.

For object $B$, $a_B = g + \dfrac{F}{m_B}$.

Since $m_A > m_B$, we have $\dfrac{F}{m_A} < \dfrac{F}{m_B}$, which implies $a_A < a_B$.

The maximum height reached by the object is $H = \dfrac{u^2}{2a}$.

Since $a_A < a_B$, we get $H_A > H_B$.

Thus, object $A$ reaches a greater height than object $B$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A smooth wedge $A$ is secured inside a chamber suspended from a fixed ceiling near the earth's surface. A block $B$ positioned at the top of the wedge requires a time $T$ to slide down the wedge's length. If the block is placed at the top of the wedge and the cable holding the chamber is snapped at that exact instant, the block will
options: stay at the top of the wedge
require a time longer than $T$ to slide down the wedge
require a time shorter than $T$ to slide down the wedge
questions.solution:
When the cable holding the chamber is snapped, the chamber and the wedge fall freely under gravity with a downward acceleration $g$.

In the non-inertial reference frame of the freely falling wedge, a pseudo force of magnitude $mg$ acts upwards on the block $B$.

The effective acceleration due to gravity in the frame of the wedge is $g_{eff} = g - g = 0$.

Since the effective gravity is zero, the normal reaction between the block and the wedge becomes zero, and there is no force component along the incline to accelerate the block down the wedge.

As the block is placed with zero initial velocity relative to the wedge, it will experience no relative acceleration and will remain at rest with respect to the wedge.

Therefore, the block will stay at the top of the wedge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an imaginary atmosphere, the air applies a small force $F$ on a particle in the direction of its motion. A particle of mass $m$ projected upward requires a time $t_1$ to reach its maximum height and a time $t_2$ for its return journey to the starting point. It follows that
options: the relation between $t_1$ and $t_2$ depends on the particle's mass
questions.solution:
During the upward journey, the particle moves upward, so the force $F$ acts upward. The gravitational force $mg$ acts downward.

The net downward acceleration during ascent is $a_1 = g - \dfrac{F}{m}$.

Let the maximum height reached be $h$. The time of ascent $t_1$ is given by the kinematic equation $h = \dfrac{1}{2} a_1 t_1^2$, which gives $t_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{a_1}}$.

During the downward journey, the particle moves downward, so the force $F$ acts downward. The gravitational force $mg$ also acts downward.

The net downward acceleration during descent is $a_2 = g + \dfrac{F}{m}$.

The time of descent $t_2$ for the same height $h$ is given by $h = \dfrac{1}{2} a_2 t_2^2$, which gives $t_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{a_2}}$.

Since $F$ is a small positive force, we have $a_1 < a_2$.

Therefore, $\sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{a_1}} > \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{a_2}}$, which implies $t_1 > t_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person standing on the floor of an elevator drops a coin. The coin reaches the elevator floor in time $t_1$ when the elevator is stationary, and in time $t_2$ when it is moving uniformly. It follows that
options: $t_1 < t_2$ or $t_1 > t_2$ depending on whether the elevator is going up or down
questions.solution:
Let the height from which the coin is dropped be $h$.

When the elevator is stationary, the initial relative velocity of the coin with respect to the elevator is zero. The relative acceleration of the coin is $g$ downwards. Using the equation of motion $S_{rel} = u_{rel} t + \dfrac{1}{2} a_{rel} t^2$, we get $h = \dfrac{1}{2} g t_1^2$, which gives $t_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{g}}$.

When the elevator is moving uniformly, its acceleration is zero. The frame of reference of the elevator is an inertial frame. The initial relative velocity of the coin with respect to the elevator is still zero, and the relative acceleration of the coin is $g$ downwards. Using the same equation of motion, we get $h = \dfrac{1}{2} g t_2^2$, which gives $t_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{g}}$.

Therefore, $t_1 = t_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A free $^{238}\text{U}$ nucleus kept inside a train emits an alpha particle. When the train is stationary, a nucleus decays, and a passenger measures the separation between the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus to be $x$ at a time $t$ after the decay. If the decay occurs while the train is moving at a uniform velocity $v$, the distance between the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus at a time $t$ after the decay, as measured by the passenger, is
options: dependent on the direction of the train
questions.solution:
The separation between the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus depends only on their relative velocity.

Let the velocity of the alpha particle be $\vec{v}_{\alpha}$ and that of the recoiling nucleus be $\vec{v}_{N}$ in the frame of the train. The relative velocity is $\vec{v}_{\text{rel}} = \vec{v}_{\alpha} - \vec{v}_{N}$.

Since the train moves with a uniform velocity $v$, it represents an inertial frame of reference. According to the principle of relativity, the laws of physics are identical in all inertial frames. The energy released in the decay and the conservation of momentum in the train's frame remain exactly the same as when the train was stationary.

Thus, the relative velocity $\vec{v}_{\text{rel}}$ between the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus is independent of the uniform velocity of the train. The separation at time $t$, given by $x = |\vec{v}_{\text{rel}}|t$, will remain unchanged.

Therefore, the distance measured by the passenger at time $t$ is $x$.
